title: Dog Eye Proptosis: Emergency Guide for an Eye Out of Socket
meta_desc: If your dog's eye has popped out of its socket, this is a critical emergency. Learn the immediate steps to take and why urgent vet care is required.
content_html:
What Is Proptosis?

Proptosis is a serious medical condition where a dog's eyeball is displaced forward, causing it to bulge out or completely exit the bony eye socket. This is most common in brachycephalic (flat-faced) breeds like Pugs, Bulldogs, and Shih Tzus, whose shallow sockets make the eye more vulnerable to trauma, such as a fall, a fight with another animal, or accidental head pressure.

Urgency Level: High Emergency

This is a critical, high-level veterinary emergency. If your dog’s eye has popped out, the optic nerve and surrounding blood vessels are being stretched and compressed, which can lead to permanent blindness or loss of the eye within minutes to hours. Do not attempt to push the eye back in yourself, as you may cause irreversible damage. Your immediate priority is to keep the eye moist with sterile saline and transport your dog to the nearest emergency clinic immediately.

How a Photo Helps Triage

While your primary focus should be on safely transporting your dog, taking a quick, clear photo of the eye can be extremely helpful for the triage staff. Sending a photo ahead or showing it to the front desk allows the veterinary team to instantly assess the severity of the displacement, identify signs of ruptured muscles or internal bleeding, and prepare the necessary surgical or stabilization equipment before you even enter the exam room.

faqs.question: Can my dog's vision be saved?
faqs.answer: Vision can sometimes be saved if the dog is treated immediately, though the prognosis depends on the severity of the trauma and how quickly the eye is surgically repositioned.

faqs.question: What should I do while driving to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog calm and prevent them from pawing at their face using an Elizabethan collar or by holding their paws. Keep the eye moist by gently placing a clean, water-soaked gauze or cloth over it.

faqs.question: Will my dog need surgery?
faqs.answer: In almost all cases of proptosis, surgery is required to either reposition the eye and suture the eyelids shut (tarsorrhaphy) or, if the damage is too severe, remove the eye entirely.
